translations.literal: Horn-the was other end-the out in sea, but there where you drank, then saw we, that decrease took in ocean-the. That are now called tides.'

original: Kötturinn var Miðgarðsormr. Þú lyftir honum svá hátt, at þú komst höfðinu upp til himins.'

translations.faulkes: The cat was the Midgard Serpent. You lifted it so high that its head reached up to heaven.'
translations.brodeur: The cat was Midgard Serpent... thou didst lift him so high that his head reached up to heaven.'
translations.literal: Cat-the was Midgard-serpent. You lifted him so high, that you came head-the up to heaven.'

original: Elli var elli sjálf. Engi maðr hefir þann kraft, at hann felli hana eigi.'

translations.faulkes: Elli was Old Age itself. No man has the strength not to be felled by her.'
translations.brodeur: Elli was Old Age; no man has the strength to stand against her.'
translations.literal: Elli was old-age self. No man has that strength, that he fells her not.'

original: Þegar Þórr heyrði þetta, greip hann hamarinn ok skyldi ljósta hann, en þá sá hann engan Utgarða-Loka ok enga borg.'

translations.faulkes: When Thor heard this, he gripped the hammer and was about to strike him, but then he saw no Utgarda-Loki and no castle.'
translations.brodeur: When Thor heard this, he gripped his hammer... but then he saw neither Útgarða-Loki nor any castle.'
translations.literal: When Thor heard this, gripped he hammer-the and should strike him, but then saw he no Utgarda-Loki and no burg.'

original: Þá sneri hann aftr til Þrúðvanga, en sá ormr lá enn í sjánum.'

translations.faulkes: Then he turned back to Thrudvangar, but that serpent still lay in the sea.'
translations.brodeur: Then he turned back to Thrúdvangar; but the serpent lay still in the sea.'
translations.literal: Then turned he back to Thrudvangar, but that serpent lay yet in sea-the.'

original: Þá mælti Gangleri: 'Mikill er Þórr fyrir sér.' Hár segir: 'Eigi er nú dult, hverr styrkr hans er.'

translations.faulkes: Then Gangleri said: 'Thor is very powerful.' High said: 'It is no longer a secret what his strength is.'
translations.brodeur: Then spake Gangleri: 'Thor is a great one... even if he were deceived.' High said: 'It is not hidden now what his strength is.'
translations.literal: Then spoke Gangleri: 'Great is Thor for himself.' High says: 'Not is now hidden, what strength his is.'

original: Þórr unni sér eigi heima ok bjóst ferðar sinnar skyndiliga, svá at hann hafði eigi kerru né hafra ok engan fylgdarman.

translations.faulkes: Thor was not content at home and prepared for a journey so hastily that he had neither carriage nor goats nor any companion.
translations.brodeur: Thor misliked his stay at home, and made ready his journey so hastily that he had with him no chariot, and no he-goats, and no company.
translations.literal: Thor loved himself not home-at [was restless] and prepared journey his quickly, so that he had not cart nor goats and no companion.

original: Hann gekk út ór Miðgarði sem ungr maðr ok kom at kveldi til jötuns þess, er Hymir er nefndr.

translations.faulkes: He went out from Midgard as a young man and came in the evening to the giant who is named Hymir.
translations.brodeur: He went out of Midgard in the guise of a young lad, and came at evening to a certain giant who is named Hymir.
translations.literal: He went out from Midgard as young man and came at evening to giant that, who Hymir is named.

original: Þórr gisti þar um nóttina. En í dagan bjóst Hymir at róa á sjá at fiska.

translations.faulkes: Thor stayed there for the night. But at dawn Hymir prepared to go out to sea to fish.
translations.brodeur: Thor tarried there as a guest that night; but at dawning Hymir rose and made ready his boat for fishing.
translations.literal: Thor lodged there over night-the. But in dawn prepared Hymir to row on sea to fish.

original: Þórr hljóp á bátinn ok bað Hymi lofa sér at róa á sjá með honum.

translations.faulkes: Thor jumped onto the boat and asked Hymir to let him row out to sea with him.
translations.brodeur: Thor sprang into the boat and prayed Hymir let him row to sea with him.
translations.literal: Thor leaped on boat-the and asked Hymir allow himself to row on sea with him.

original: Þórr gekk þar til, er hann sá yxnflokk nokkurn. Hann tók þann yxinn, er Himinhrjóðr hét, ok sleit af höfuðit.

translations.faulkes: Thor went to where he saw a herd of oxen. He took that ox called Himinhrjod and tore off its head.
translations.brodeur: Thor went thither where he saw a certain herd of oxen... he took that ox which was called Himinhrjódr, and struck off its head.
translations.literal: Thor went there to, where he saw ox-flock some. He took that ox-the, which Himinhrjod named, and tore off head-the.

original: Hymir mælti, at þeir væru komnir nógu langt, en Þórr vildi róa miklu lengra.

translations.faulkes: Hymir said that they had come far enough, but Thor wanted to row much further.
translations.brodeur: Hymir said that they had come far enough... but Thor said he would row much further.
translations.literal: Hymir spoke, that they were come enough far, but Thor wished row much further.

original: Hymir mælti: 'Hér munu vér komnir á þær slóðir, er Miðgarðsormr liggr.'

translations.faulkes: Hymir said: 'We must have come to those waters where the Midgard Serpent lies.'
translations.brodeur: Hymir spake: 'We are come so far that it is perilous to sit out longer because of the Midgard Serpent.'
translations.literal: Hymir spoke: 'Here will we come on those tracks, where Midgard-serpent lies.'

original: Þórr tók þá vaðinn ok hnefldist við oxahöfuðit ok kastaði fyrir borð. Miðgarðsormr geinaði yfir oxahöfuðinu.

translations.faulkes: Thor then took the line and fixed the ox-head on the hook and threw it overboard. The Midgard Serpent gaped over the ox-head.
translations.brodeur: Thor took a line... and set the ox-head on the hook and cast it overboard; and the Midgard Serpent gaped at the ox-head.'
translations.literal: Thor took then line-the and fixed with ox-head-the and threw before board. Midgard-serpent gaped over ox-head-the.

original: En er ormrinn kastaði við, þá hnykti Þórr svá fast, at báðir hnefar hans skullu á borðinu.

translations.faulkes: And when the serpent jerked away, Thor pulled so hard that both his fists struck the gunwale.
translations.brodeur: And when the Serpent felt the hook, he tugged so hard that both Thor's fists struck the gunwale.'
translations.literal: And when serpent-the jerked back, then snatched Thor so fast, that both fists his struck on board-the.

original: Þórr spyrnti svá fast, at hann hljóp í gegnum bátinn ok stóð á grunni.

translations.faulkes: Thor kicked so hard that his feet went through the boat and he stood on the sea-bed.
translations.brodeur: Thor braced his feet so hard that he thrust through the boat with his feet and stepped on the bottom.'
translations.literal: Thor kicked so fast, that he leaped through boat-the and stood on bottom.

original: Þórr greip hamarinn ok hóf á loft, en jötunninn hljóp til hnífs síns ok hjó á vaðinn.

translations.faulkes: Thor gripped the hammer and raised it aloft, but the giant rushed for his knife and cut the line.
translations.brodeur: Thor gripped his hammer and raised it aloft; but the giant fumbled for his fishing-knife and hacked off the line.'
translations.literal: Thor gripped hammer-the and raised on aloft, but giant-the leaped to knife his and hewed on line-the.

original: Ormrinn sökk í sjáinn, en Þórr kastaði hamrinum eftir honum.

translations.faulkes: The serpent sank into the sea, but Thor threw the hammer after it.
translations.brodeur: The Serpent sank into the sea, but Thor cast his hammer after him.'
translations.literal: Serpent-the sank in sea-the, but Thor threw hammer-the after him.

original: Þórr laust Hymi svá mikit högg, at hann fell fyrir borð, en Þórr vað til lands.

translations.faulkes: Thor struck Hymir such a blow that he fell overboard, but Thor waded to land.
translations.brodeur: Thor dealt Hymir a blow... so that he fell overboard... but Thor waded to land.'
translations.literal: Thor struck Hymir so great blow, that he fell before board, but Thor waded to land.

original: Þá mælti Gangleri: 'Hvat er næst þessu at segja?' Hár svarar: 'Frá Baldri eru þau tíðindi, er mest eru.'

translations.faulkes: Then Gangleri said: 'What is the next thing to tell?' High replied: 'The news of Baldr is the most important.'
translations.brodeur: Then spake Gangleri: 'What more is there to tell?' High answered: 'There are tidings which are of greatest import... concerning Baldr.'
translations.literal: Then spoke Gangleri: 'What is next this to say?' High answers: 'From Baldr are those tidings, which most are.'

original: Baldr inn góða dreymdi drauma stóra ok hættliga um líf sitt.

translations.faulkes: Baldr the Good dreamed great and dangerous dreams about his life.
translations.brodeur: Baldr the Good dreamed great and perilous dreams touching his life.'
translations.literal: Baldr the good dreamed dreams great and dangerous about life his.

original: Frigg tók eiða til þess, at hlífa Baldri, af eldi ok vatni, af járni ok öllum málmi, af steinum ok jörðu, af trjánum, af sóttum, af dýrunum.

translations.faulkes: Frigg took oaths to spare Baldr from fire and water, from iron and all metals, from stones and earth, from trees, from diseases, from animals.
translations.brodeur: Frigg took oaths to spare Baldr from fire and water, from iron and all kinds of metal, from stones, earth, trees, sicknesses, beasts.'
translations.literal: Frigg took oaths to that, to spare Baldr, from fire and water, from iron and all metal, from stones and earth, from trees-the, from sicknesses, from animals-the.

original: Þat var þá skemmtun ásanna, at Baldr skyldi standa upp á þingum, en allir aðrir skyldu skjóta á hann eða höggva.

translations.faulkes: It became an amusement for the Aesir that Baldr should stand at assemblies and everyone else should shoot at him or strike him.
translations.brodeur: This was the pastime of the Aesir... that Baldr should stand up at the Thing... and all the others should shoot at him or hew at him.'
translations.literal: That was then amusement of-Aesir, that Baldr should stand up on assemblies, but all others should shoot on him or hew.

original: En er Loki sá þetta, þá líkaði honum illa, er Baldr sakaði ekki. Hann brá á sik konu líki.

translations.faulkes: But when Loki saw this, he was displeased that Baldr was not harmed. He assumed the form of a woman.
translations.brodeur: But when Loki saw this, it misliked him... that Baldr took no hurt. He took on him the likeness of a woman.'
translations.literal: But when Loki saw this, then liked-it him ill, that Baldr hurt not. He cast on himself woman's likeness.

original: Loki spurði Frigg, ef allir hlutir hefðu eiða svarit. Frigg svarar: 'Vaxinn er einn viðarteinungr fyrir vestan Valhöll. Sá er mistilteinn kallaðr.'

translations.faulkes: Loki asked Frigg if all things had sworn oaths. Frigg replied: 'There is a twig growing west of Valhalla called mistletoe.'
translations.brodeur: Loki asked Frigg if all things had sworn oaths... Frigg answered: 'There grows a tree-sprout alone... it is called Mistletoe.'
translations.literal: Loki asked Frigg, if all things had oaths sworn. Frigg answers: 'Grown is one wood-twig before west Valhalla. That is mistletoe called.'

original: Loki tók mistilteininn ok sleit upp ok gekk til þings.

translations.faulkes: Loki took the mistletoe and tore it up and went to the assembly.
translations.brodeur: Loki took the Mistletoe and pulled it up and went to the Thing.'
translations.literal: Loki took mistletoe-the and tore up and went to assembly.

original: Höðr tók mistilteininn ok skaut at Baldri at tilvísun Loka. Fleinninn flaug í gönum honum ok fell hann dauðr til jarðar.

translations.faulkes: Hod took the mistletoe and shot at Baldr at Loki's direction. The shaft flew through him and he fell dead to the ground.
translations.brodeur: Hödr took the Mistletoe and shot at Baldr, Loki guiding his hand. The shaft flew through him, and he fell dead to the earth.
translations.literal: Hod took mistletoe-the and shot at Baldr at direction of-Loki. Shaft-the flew through him and fell he dead to earth.

original: Þá er Baldr var fallinn, þá féllust öllum ásum orðtök ok svá hendur at taka til hans.

translations.faulkes: When Baldr had fallen, all the Aesir lost the power of speech and also the use of their hands to pick him up.
translations.brodeur: When Baldr was fallen, words failed all the Aesir, and their hands failed also to clasp him.
translations.literal: When that Baldr was fallen, then failed all Aesir words and also hands to take to him.

original: Óðinn bar þenna skaða svá mikinn, at hann vissi mest, hversu mikit afnám ok skaði ásunum var í fráfalli Baldurs.

translations.faulkes: Odin felt this loss most severely, for he understood best what a great deprivation and loss the death of Baldr was for the Aesir.
translations.brodeur: Odin took this misfortune most heavily, for he best perceived what a great deprivation and loss the death of Baldr was to the Aesir.
translations.literal: Odin bore this loss so great, that he knew most, how great deprivation and loss to-Aesir-the was in death of-Baldr.

original: Frigg spurði, hverr væri sá með ásum, er ríða vildi á Helveg ok freista ef hann mætti finna Baldr.

translations.faulkes: Frigg asked who there was among the Aesir who would ride the road to Hel and try to find Baldr.
translations.brodeur: Frigg asked who there might be among the Aesir who would ride the Way to Hel and seek to find Baldr.
translations.literal: Frigg asked, who were that-one with Aesir, who ride would on Hel-way and try if he might find Baldr.

original: Hermóðr inn hvati, sonr Óðins, nefndist til þeirar ferðar. Hann tók Sleipni, hest Óðins, ok steig á ok hleypst af stað.

translations.faulkes: Hermod the Bold, Odin's son, offered himself for the journey. He took Sleipnir, Odin's horse, and mounted and galloped away.
translations.brodeur: Hermóðr the Bold, Odin's son, was named for that journey. He took Sleipnir, Odin's horse, and mounted him, and leaped forth on his way.
translations.literal: Hermod the bold, son of-Odin, named-himself to that journey. He took Sleipnir, horse of-Odin, and stepped on and leaped from stead [set off].

original: Hermóðr reið níu nætur dökka dali ok djúpa, svá at hann sá ekki, fyrr en hann kom til árinnar Gjallar.

translations.faulkes: Hermod rode for nine nights through dark and deep valleys, so that he saw nothing until he reached the river Gjöll.
translations.brodeur: Hermóðr rode nine nights through dark dales and deep... until he came to the river Gjöll.
translations.literal: Hermod rode nine nights dark valleys and deep, so that he saw not, before than he came to river-the Gjoll.

original: Mær sú gætti brúarinnar, er Móðguðr heitir. Hón spurði hann at nafni ok ætt.

translations.faulkes: A maiden called Modgud guarded the bridge. She asked him his name and lineage.
translations.brodeur: The maiden who guards the bridge is named Móðguðr; she asked him his name and race.
translations.literal: Maiden that guarded bridge-the, who Modgud named. She asked him at name and kin.

original: Hermóðr reið þar til, er hann kom at Helgrindum. Hann hleypti hestinum yfir grindina.

translations.faulkes: Hermod rode until he reached the gates of Hel. He made the horse leap over the gate.
translations.brodeur: Hermóðr rode on till he came to Hel-gate... he gave the spurs to the horse, and it leaped so hard over the gate.
translations.literal: Hermod rode there until, when he came at Hel-gates. He leaped horse-the over gate-the.

original: Hann sá þar Baldr, bróður sinn, sitja í öndugi. Hermóðr dvaldist þar um nóttina.

translations.faulkes: He saw his brother Baldr sitting there in the place of honor. Hermod stayed there for the night.
translations.brodeur: He saw Baldr, his brother, sitting in the high-seat... Hermóðr tarried there that night.
translations.literal: He saw there Baldr, brother his, sit in high-seat. Hermod stayed there over night-the.

original: Um morguninn bað Hermóðr Hel, at Baldr skyldi ríða heim með honum. Hel sagði, at reyna skyldi, ef Baldr væri svá ástsaell sem sagt er.

translations.faulkes: In the morning Hermod asked Hel that Baldr might ride home with him. Hel said it should be tested whether Baldr was as beloved as was said.
translations.brodeur: At morn Hermóðr prayed Hel that Baldr might ride home with him... Hel said that this should be put to the proof, whether Baldr were so all-beloved as is said.
translations.literal: Around morning-the asked Hermod Hel, that Baldr should ride home with him. Hel said, that try should, if Baldr were so beloved as said is.

original: 'Ef allir hlutir í veröldinni, kykvir ok dauðir, gráta hann, þá skal hann fara aftr til ásanna.'

translations.faulkes: 'If everything in the world, living and dead, weeps for him, then he shall go back to the Aesir.'
translations.brodeur: 'If all things in the world, quick and dead, weep for him, then he shall go back to the Aesir.'
translations.literal: 'If all things in world-the, living and dead, weep him, then shall he go back to Aesir-the.'

original: Æsirnir sendu um allan heim erindsreka at biðja, at Baldr væri grátinn ór Helju.

translations.faulkes: The Aesir sent messengers all over the world to request that Baldr be wept out of Hel.
translations.brodeur: Then the Aesir sent over all the world messengers to pray that Baldr might be wept out of Hel.
translations.literal: Aesir-the sent around all world messengers to ask, that Baldr were wept out-of Hel.

original: En er sendimenn fóru heim, fundu þeir í helli nokkurum hlandskonu, er nefndist Þökk. Þeir báðu hana gráta Baldr.

translations.faulkes: But as the messengers were returning home, they found a giantess in a cave called Thokk. They asked her to weep for Baldr.
translations.brodeur: When the messengers went home... they found in a certain cave a giantess; she called herself Thökk. They prayed her to weep Baldr out of Hel.
translations.literal: But when messengers went home, found they in cave some giantess, who named-was Thokk. They asked her weep Baldr.

original: Hón svarar: 'Þökk mun gráta þurrum tárum Baldurs bálför. Hvorki kviks né dauðs naut ek karls sonar. Haldi Hel því, er hefir.'

translations.faulkes: She replied: 'Thokk will weep dry tears for Baldr's funeral. I had no use for the old man's son, living or dead. Let Hel hold what she has.'
translations.brodeur: She answered: 'Thökk will weep with dry tears Baldr's bale-fire... let Hel hold what she hath.'
translations.literal: She answers: 'Thokk will weep dry tears Baldr's bale-journey. Neither quick nor dead used I man's son. Hold Hel that, which has.'

original: En þess geta menn, at þar hafi verið Loki Laufeyjarson, er flest illt hefir gert með ásum.

translations.faulkes: And it is thought that this must have been Loki Laufeyjarson, who has done most evil among the Aesir.
translations.brodeur: And men ween that there was Loki Laufeyjarson, who hath wrought most ill among the Aesir.
translations.literal: But that guess men, that there had been Loki Laufeyjarson, who most ill has done with Aesir.

original: Þá varð Loki tekinn ok færðr í helli nokkurn. Þeir tóku þrjá hellusteina ok settu á egg ok boruðu rauf í stein hvern.

translations.faulkes: Then Loki was taken and brought to a certain cave. They took three flat stones and set them on edge and drilled a hole through each stone.
translations.brodeur: Then Loki was taken... and brought to a certain cave. They took three flat stones, and set them on edge and drilled a hole through each stone.
translations.literal: Then was Loki taken and brought in cave some. They took three cave-stones and set on edge and bored hole in stone each.

original: Skaði tók eitrhorm ok festi upp yfir andlit Loka. Sigyn, kona hans, stendr hjá honum ok heldr mundlaug undir eitrdropana.

translations.faulkes: Skadi took a venomous serpent and fastened it up over Loki's face. Sigyn, his wife, stands by him and holds a basin under the drops of venom.
translations.brodeur: Skaði took a venomous serpent and fastened it up over Loki's face... Sigyn, his wife, stands by him and holds a basin under the venom-drops.
translations.literal: Skadi took venom-serpent and fastened up over face of-Loki. Sigyn, woman his, stands by him and holds basin under venom-drops-the.

original: En er mundlaugin fyllist, þá fer hón ok hverfir ór eitrinu, en meðan drýpur eitrið í andlit honum. Þá kippist hann svá hart við, at jörð öll skelfr.

translations.faulkes: But when the basin is full, she goes and empties out the venom, and meanwhile the venom drips onto his face. Then he jerks so hard that the whole earth shakes.
translations.brodeur: But when the basin is full... then the venom drips on his face; then he writhes against it so hard that all the earth trembles.'
translations.literal: But when basin-the fills-itself, then goes she and turns [empties] out venom-the, but meanwhile drips venom-the in face him. Then jerks he so hard with, that earth all shakes.

original: Þá mælti Gangleri: 'Hvat er þá at segja frá ragnarökum?' Hár svarar: 'Margt ok stórt er frá því at segja.'

translations.faulkes: Then Gangleri said: 'What is to be said about Ragnarok?' High replied: 'There are many and great things to tell about it.'
translations.brodeur: Then spake Gangleri: 'What tidings are to be told concerning Ragnarök?' High answered: 'Many things there are to be said thereof, and great.'
translations.literal: Then spoke Gangleri: 'What is then to say from ragnarok?' High answers: 'Much and great is from that to say.'

original: Ok þegar heyrði Gangleri gný mikinn til allra handa ok leit út úr höllinni. Þá sá hann enga höll ok enga borg.

translations.faulkes: And immediately Gangleri heard a great noise on all sides and looked out of the hall. Then he saw no hall and no castle.
translations.brodeur: Straightway Gangleri heard a great noise on every side... he saw no hall and no castle, but only the open plain.'
translations.literal: And at-once heard Gangleri noise great to all hands [sides] and looked out from hall-the. Then saw he no hall and no burg.
